title: Nadia vs Evangelion
wikitext:
[]: Nadia vs Evangelion             Quote Post by ApolloIVtv » Mon Apr 14, 2014 1:00 am This is something I'm surprised a lot of people don't talk about. Maybe you guys talked about on here before, but I didn't find anything about it, let alone on Google. I know there are some comparison sites, but they're not analytical comparisons. The reason I find it strange is that it's one of Anno's first anime as a director, and also very positively received. Also there's a bunch of similarities as well, little references and character arcs that are somewhat the same. Some shots are the same, some music (Sagisu also did Nadia's music before Eva). The complexities of the characters and their relationships with others play a big factor in both of shows as well. I know it's not entirely Anno's story though, the concept belongs to Miyazaki, and it's also not his most personal or daring work, but it's a pretty solid piece, and I'm surprised Nadia itself isn't really talked about as much, being an anime from the all mighty powerful Hideaki Anno. So, I guess I'll just bring it up here. Which do you guys, the ones who've seen both well enough, think is better. But I'm not talking just story-wise though? If we did, this might be over a little too soon. What I mean is also the overall impact, with a completely objective viewpoint of both shows, as well characters are better and their arcs and such. I guess another thing we could discuss is how Evangelion shadowed over Nadia and why it did, etc. I may not be the one to really ask all this, but I'm honestly curious on your guy's opinion.

[]:             Quote Post by The Eva Monkey » Mon Apr 14, 2014 12:52 pm It is a shame, Nadia is essentially the prototype for Evangelion, so any serious fan of Evangelion who wants to better understand it as a creative work really ought to see Nadia at least once. People think Evangelion was cool or novel for its use of Judeo-Christian mythology, but Anno had already done it with Nadia. Also, fuck the island arc.

[]:             Quote Post by pwhodges » Mon Apr 14, 2014 1:10 pm The Eva Monkey wrote: Also, fuck the island arc.

[]: Re: Nadia vs Evangelion             Quote Post by SEELE » Mon Apr 14, 2014 1:14 pm ApolloIVtv wrote: [...] I guess another thing we could discuss is how Evangelion shadowed over Nadia and why it did, etc. I may not be the one to really ask all this, but I'm honestly curious on your guy's opinion.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Mon Apr 14, 2014 3:13 pm I saw Nadia first. I loved it, sans Island and Africa arcs. (In fact, due to Nadia’s generally uplifting tone, I was kinda expecting NGE to be more like what Pacific Rim ended up becoming: Bad-ass robot fights with a stronger attachment to the core characters than usual and a fun time to be had by the viewers.) Nadia’s OP is still one of my all-time favorites, right up there with Bebop’s Tank and Black-Lagoon’s OP. And despite Nadia’s ED being the same in every episode, the last time it’s played is the only time ever that an ED somehow makes me feel nostalgic over a certain time in the characters’ lives that I saw only 5 minutes ago in the exact same episode. Also, a note on the Island arc: As I watch more Japanese cinema, I’m learning that I probably shouldn’t put all of the blame on the terribleness of the Island Arc on Shinji Higuchi, the series’ stand-in director at that point in production. His visual effects shots in the Gamera films generally stands head-and-shoulder’s above the shots filmed by the dramatic director himself, and his directorial work on the God Warrior’s short film was quite gripping even without Anno’s outstanding effects work. I honestly can’t wait to see what Higuchi does as director of the live-action Attack on Titan movie. Nadia’s Island Arc was just a result of the production being stuck between a rock and a hard place, with NHK and TOHO demanding more episodes and the creative team at GAINAX scrambling to deliver something in time. So, the message an Evangelion fan should gleam from watching the Island Arc of Nadia is that one shouldn’t blame everything on Shinji, no matter how much you might want to.

[]:             Quote Post by BrikHaus » Sun Apr 20, 2014 2:18 pm Evangelion is definitely the better work as Anno has more experience and is a more mature director than he was when he worked on Nadia. Nadia is better for lighter/happy entertainment. Evangelion is better for richer characters, more intricate story, and seriously WTF moments.

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Sun Apr 20, 2014 5:04 pm Nadia is fantastic series and one of the most beloved 90s anime in Japan. As work of adventure genre it's only topped by Miyazaki's Laputa and Future Boy Conan. But then there's that fucking awful, outsourced cour worth of filler that really drives would-be masterpiece to the ground. Don't get me wrong, Nadia is still great series. But there's no way it ever measures up to Eva as long as that blemish exists. This would be the case even if Nadia was as good across the board as Eva otherwise (it's not). I do disagree with Brik it has anything to do with Anno's maturity as a director. Even as a person he seems to have changed very little from 1991 interviews. It's just different artistic intentions and circumstances - it's no secret Anno didn't exactly have free hands on Nadia even though he overstepped his boundaries vis-a-vis NHK a lot. With NGE he just flipped a finger at the whole system like he had already wanted to do with Nadia. edit: And yeah it's not really fair to put blame for Island/Africa arc squarely on Higuchi's shoulders. As said that stuff was to large extent outsourced and production meddling wasn't uncommon (the infamous musical episode came to be at last minute simply because NHK forced them to market upcoming character song CDs! That episode was hastily put together by GAINAX folks - Anno included - under fake korean names.) Higuchi is extremely talented SFX man and storyboard artist. In that regard he has always been Anno's go-to guy since the beginning (Gunbuster was basically co-storyboarded by Anno and Higuchi from start to end). I can't evaluate him as director clearly because I haven't really seen any of his feature films - though based on what I've heard Japan Sinks should be ok catastrophe film.

[]:             Quote Post by C.A.P. » Sun Apr 20, 2014 8:13 pm Wasn't Sadamoto the director of the first two episodes of Nadia?

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Mon Apr 21, 2014 7:50 am C.A.P. wrote: Wasn't Sadamoto the director of the first two episodes of Nadia?

[]:             Quote Post by pwhodges » Mon Apr 21, 2014 8:13 am Wikipedia tells the story (references linked in Wikipedia): Wikipedia wrote: Gainax's initial involvement with the project occurred during an internal power struggle within the company
